37	// pack-level unpack failure remains fatal.
38	//
39	// Returned by NewPusher as a pointer so callers can attach OnRejection after
40	// construction without worrying about whether downstream strategies have
41	// already captured a value copy.
42	type Pusher struct {
43		Conn        Conn
44		Adv         *packp.AdvRefs
45		Verbose     bool
46		OnRejection func(refName plumbing.ReferenceName, status string)
47	
48		// MaxRefUpdates caps ref-update commands per receive-pack request. Zero
49		// uses the env-or-default limit (see MaxRefUpdatesEnv); a positive value
50		// overrides it — e.g. from the --target-max-ref-updates flag.
51		MaxRefUpdates int
52	}
53	
54	// NewPusher builds a target-side push executor.
55	func NewPusher(conn Conn, adv *packp.AdvRefs, verbose bool) *Pusher {
56		return &Pusher{Conn: conn, Adv: adv, Verbose: verbose}
57	}
58	
59	// defaultMaxRefUpdatesPerPush bounds how many ref-update commands ride in a
60	// single receive-pack request. The default is deliberately conservative:
61	// GitHub returns 500 Internal Server Error when a single push updates ~10k refs
62	// at once but accepts 5k, so 5_000 mirrors a many-ref repo there without
63	// tripping its (undocumented) ceiling. entire-server tolerates far more — its
64	// hard cap is 25_000 (server/githttp.maxRefUpdateCommands) — so trusted callers
65	// pushing to entire-server raise this via MaxRefUpdatesEnv to cut round trips.
66	//
67	// Splitting is safe because the pack accompanying the first batch carries every
68	// object for the whole push: receive-pack commits the entire received pack into
69	// the object store (entire-server via CommitQuarantinedFanout, canonical git via
70	// tmp_objdir_migrate — neither prunes objects unreachable from the pushed tips),
71	// so the remaining batches only need to move ref pointers and carry no pack.
72	const defaultMaxRefUpdatesPerPush = 5_000
73	
74	// MaxRefUpdatesEnv overrides defaultMaxRefUpdatesPerPush with a positive
75	// integer. Raise it for targets known to accept large ref-update pushes (e.g.
76	// entire-server, up to its 25_000 cap) to reduce round trips; lower it for a
77	// provider that rejects even the default. Invalid or non-positive values fall
78	// back to the default.
79	const MaxRefUpdatesEnv = "GITSYNC_MAX_REF_UPDATES_PER_PUSH"
80	
81	// maxRefUpdatesPerPush is resolved once from the environment so the limit can be
82	// tuned per target without rebuilding (see MaxRefUpdatesEnv).
83	var maxRefUpdatesPerPush = resolveMaxRefUpdatesPerPush()
84	
85	func resolveMaxRefUpdatesPerPush() int {
86		if v := os.Getenv(MaxRefUpdatesEnv); v != "" {
87			if n, err := strconv.Atoi(v); err == nil && n > 0 {
88				return n
89			}
90		}
91		return defaultMaxRefUpdatesPerPush
92	}
93	
94	// effectiveMaxRefUpdates resolves a per-push limit: a positive override wins,
95	// otherwise the env-or-default limit applies.
96	func effectiveMaxRefUpdates(maxRefUpdates int) int {
97		if maxRefUpdates > 0 {
98			return maxRefUpdates
99		}
100		return maxRefUpdatesPerPush
101	}
102	
103	// chunkRefUpdates splits commands into batches no larger than limit. Input that
104	// already fits is returned as a single batch (including the empty slice, so
105	// callers preserve their one-request behavior).
106	func chunkRefUpdates(commands []PushCommand, limit int) [][]PushCommand {
107		if len(commands) <= limit {
108			return [][]PushCommand{commands}
109		}
110		batches := make([][]PushCommand, 0, (len(commands)+limit-1)/limit)
111		for start := 0; start < len(commands); start += limit {
112			end := min(start+limit, len(commands))
113			batches = append(batches, commands[start:end])
114		}
115		return batches
116	}
117	
118	// splitFirstBatch peels off the first batch (up to limit) so a push can carry
119	// the pack with that batch and send the remainder as ref-only follow-ups. rest
120	// is nil when commands already fit in a single request.
121	func splitFirstBatch(commands []PushCommand, limit int) (first, rest []PushCommand) {
122		if len(commands) <= limit {
123			return commands, nil
124		}
125		return commands[:limit], commands[limit:]
126	}
127	
128	// logRefUpdateBatch reports completion of one ref-update batch to the progress
129	// writer. Ref-only follow-up batches push with progress suppressed (their
130	// sideband carries nothing but a bare "target:" line per batch), so this is the
131	// only per-batch signal; it stays quiet unless verbose and the push actually
132	// spanned multiple batches.
133	func logRefUpdateBatch(conn Conn, verbose bool, batchNum, totalBatches, refs int) {
134		if !verbose || totalBatches <= 1 {
135			return
136		}
137		w := conn.ProgressWriter()
138		if w == nil {
139			w = os.Stderr
140		}
141		fmt.Fprintf(w, "target: pushed ref-update batch %d/%d (%d refs)\n", batchNum, totalBatches, refs)
142	}
143	
144	// PushPack streams a pack to the target.
145	func (p *Pusher) PushPack(ctx context.Context, commands []PushCommand, pack io.ReadCloser) error {
146		return PushPack(ctx, p.Conn, p.Adv, commands, pack, p.MaxRefUpdates, p.Verbose, p.OnRejection)
147	}
148	
149	// PushCommands sends ref-only updates. Creates/updates carry an empty pack;
150	// delete-only pushes carry no pack. See the package-level PushCommands.
151	func (p *Pusher) PushCommands(ctx context.Context, commands []PushCommand) error {
152		return PushCommands(ctx, p.Conn, p.Adv, commands, p.MaxRefUpdates, p.Verbose, p.OnRejection)
153	}
154	
155	// PushObjects encodes and pushes locally materialized objects.
156	func (p *Pusher) PushObjects(ctx context.Context, commands []PushCommand, store storer.Storer, hashes []plumbing.Hash) error {
157		return PushObjects(ctx, p.Conn, p.Adv, commands, store, hashes, p.MaxRefUpdates, p.Verbose, p.OnRejection)
158	}
159	
160	// buildUpdateRequest builds the receive-pack update request.
161	func buildUpdateRequest(
162		adv *packp.AdvRefs,
163		commands []PushCommand,
164		verbose bool,
165	) (*packp.UpdateRequests, bool, bool, error) {
166		req := &packp.UpdateRequests{}
167		if sb := PreferredSideband(&adv.Capabilities); sb != "" {
168			req.Capabilities.Set(sb)
169		}
170		if adv.Capabilities.Supports(capability.ReportStatus) {
171			req.Capabilities.Set(capability.ReportStatus)
172		}
173	
174		hasDelete := false
175		hasUpdates := false
176		for _, cmd := range commands {
177			c := &packp.Command{Name: cmd.Name, Old: cmd.Old}
178			if cmd.Delete {
179				c.New = plumbing.ZeroHash
180				hasDelete = true
181			} else {
182				c.New = cmd.New
183				hasUpdates = true
184			}
185			req.Commands = append(req.Commands, c)
186		}
187	
188		if hasDelete {
189			if !adv.Capabilities.Supports(capability.DeleteRefs) {
190				return nil, false, false, errors.New("target does not support delete-refs")
191			}
192			req.Capabilities.Set(capability.DeleteRefs)
193		}
194	
195		_ = verbose // progress handling is server-side in HTTP mode
196		return req, hasDelete, hasUpdates, nil
197	}
198	
199	// leaseFailureMarkers are receive-pack ng reason substrings that indicate the
200	// captured target tip didn't match what was on the server at push time. Match
201	// is case-insensitive. CommandStatusErr.Status is a free-form string in go-git,
202	// so substring matching is the only option absent upstream sentinels.
203	var leaseFailureMarkers = []string{
204		"stale info",
205		"fetch first",
206		"non-fast-forward",
207		"does not match",
208	}
209	
210	// IsLeaseFailure reports whether a receive-pack ng reason indicates the
211	// captured target tip no longer matched at push time. Callers that downgrade
212	// per-ref rejections to warnings (BestEffort) must still treat these as fatal
213	// to preserve --force-with-lease semantics.
214	func IsLeaseFailure(status string) bool {
215		lowered := strings.ToLower(status)
216		for _, marker := range leaseFailureMarkers {
217			if strings.Contains(lowered, marker) {
218				return true
219			}
220		}
221		return false
222	}
223	
224	// annotateLeaseFailure wraps a lease-failure CommandStatusErr with a retry/
225	// override hint. Other receive-pack errors pass through unchanged.
226	func annotateLeaseFailure(err error) error {
227		cs, ok := commandStatusErr(err)
228		if !ok {
229			return err
230		}
231		if !IsLeaseFailure(cs.Status) {
232			return err
233		}
234		return fmt.Errorf("%w (target ref %s moved or differs from session start; rerun, or use --force-blind to overwrite)", err, cs.ReferenceName)
235	}
236	
237	// ErrTargetRefMoved is reported (wrapped) when a push to the target was rejected
238	// because the target ref changed concurrently between this run's plan and its
239	// push — a benign, retryable compare-and-swap / lease miss rather than a real
240	// failure. Test for it with errors.Is(err, ErrTargetRefMoved); the concrete
241	// error in the chain is a *RefRejectedError. Re-exported publicly as
242	// gitsync.ErrTargetRefMoved.
243	var ErrTargetRefMoved = errors.New("target ref moved concurrently")
244	
245	// RefRejectedError is a single per-ref "ng" status returned by the target's
246	// receive-pack report-status. Ref is the rejected ref; Reason is the raw,
247	// server-defined reason text — the git wire protocol carries no structured error
248	// code, so Reason is free-form and server-specific. Reach it with errors.As.
249	// Rejections git-sync can prove are concurrent target-ref moves additionally
250	// satisfy errors.Is(err, ErrTargetRefMoved), letting callers branch on the cause
251	// without substring-matching Reason themselves. Re-exported publicly as
252	// gitsync.RefRejectedError.
253	//
254	// When a single push rejects multiple refs, report-status surfaces the first
255	// failing ref (go-git follows canonical git here), so Ref/Reason reflect that
256	// first ref; any others resurface on the next attempt. The ErrTargetRefMoved
257	// classification cannot be reproduced by external construction (the deciding
258	// field is unexported by design) — to exercise errors.Is in a downstream test,
259	// wrap the sentinel directly: fmt.Errorf("...: %w", gitsync.ErrTargetRefMoved).
260	type RefRejectedError struct {
261		Ref    string // the rejected ref, e.g. "refs/heads/main"
262		Reason string // raw receive-pack ng reason, e.g. "remote ref has changed"
263	
264		moved bool  // git-sync's mode-independent judgment: an unambiguous concurrent move
265		err   error // underlying error; preserves *packp.CommandStatusErr (+ any lease-hint annotation)
266	}
267	
268	// Error is safe on a zero-value/externally-constructed RefRejectedError (one
269	// with no wrapped err), so embedders can build &RefRejectedError{Ref, Reason}
270	// in tests without a nil panic.
271	func (e *RefRejectedError) Error() string {
272		if e.err == nil {
273			return fmt.Sprintf("ref %s rejected: %s", e.Ref, e.Reason)
274		}
275		return e.err.Error()
276	}
277	
278	// Unwrap exposes the underlying receive-pack error so existing
279	// errors.As(*packp.CommandStatusErr) checks — and substring inspection of the
280	// message — keep working byte-for-byte for callers that have not migrated.
281	func (e *RefRejectedError) Unwrap() error { return e.err }
282	
283	// Is matches ErrTargetRefMoved only when this rejection is a concurrent
284	// target-ref move. Other rejections remain reachable via errors.As but are not
285	// ErrTargetRefMoved.
286	func (e *RefRejectedError) Is(target error) bool {
287		return target == ErrTargetRefMoved && e.moved
288	}
289	
290	// concurrentMoveMarkers are receive-pack ng reasons that UNAMBIGUOUSLY mean the
291	// target ref changed under us between plan and push — a clean compare-and-swap /
292	// lease miss that a plain retry resolves. "Changed" covers both an existing ref
293	// moving to a new tip AND a planned-absent ref appearing: each is a concurrent
294	// push of the same repo winning the race, and each self-heals on the next run
295	// (re-plan against the new state usually no-ops). Deliberately NARROWER than
296	// leaseFailureMarkers: "non-fast-forward" / "fetch first" are excluded because an
297	// update that is legitimately non-fast-forward and wasn't force-pushed looks
298	// identical to a race, and treating it as a benign move would mask a real
299	// "needs --force" failure.
300	//
301	// This set is server-specific by design. "remote ref has changed" is
302	// entire-server's update-side compare-and-swap rejection
303	// (storage.ErrReferenceHasChanged): the planned old hash no longer matches the
304	// target tip. "already exists" is the same CAS on the create side — git-sync
305	// only emits a create command (old = zero hash) for a ref it found ABSENT at
306	// plan time, so the server reporting it present at push time can only mean a
307	// concurrent sync created it first. That makes "already exists" exactly as
308	// unambiguous a race as "remote ref has changed", just for the create rather
309	// than the update path; both are the rejections git-sync's own targets emit, and
310	// the cases that matter in practice. (entire-server's bootstrap-mode planner has
311	// a separate "target ref ... already exists" *planning* error, but that never
312	// reaches receive-pack report-status, so it can't surface here.)
313	//
314	// "stale info" is git's force-with-lease lease-miss phrasing, kept for
315	// consistency with leaseFailureMarkers and defence-in-depth; note it is
316	// primarily a client-side status, so it may not arrive as a server ng reason on
317	// every target. Stock git servers phrase a CAS miss differently again
318	// ("failed to update ref" / "cannot lock ref ... but expected ..."), which this
319	// set does NOT match — so against a non-entire target a genuine race may fall
320	// through to a plain rejection. Extend this set as new server phrasings are
321	// observed. Match is case-insensitive substring (Reason is free-form; see
322	// RefRejectedError).
323	var concurrentMoveMarkers = []string{
324		"remote ref has changed",
325		"already exists",
326		"stale info",
327	}
328	
329	// isConcurrentMove reports whether a receive-pack ng reason is an unambiguous
330	// concurrent target-ref move (see concurrentMoveMarkers).
331	func isConcurrentMove(reason string) bool {
332		lowered := strings.ToLower(reason)
333		for _, marker := range concurrentMoveMarkers {
334			if strings.Contains(lowered, marker) {
335				return true
336			}
337		}
338		return false
339	}
340	
341	// commandStatusErr extracts go-git's per-ref CommandStatusErr from err's chain.
342	// errors.As is EXACT about value-vs-pointer, and the form go-git uses is not
343	// part of its API contract: today report.Error() returns the error BY VALUE
344	// (value receiver Error(), constructed by value in report_status.go), but go-git
345	// is an alpha dependency and could switch to a *CommandStatusErr. A target that
346	// only matches one form would silently stop classifying every rejection if the
347	// other showed up — the exact failure a pointer-only target caused before. So we
348	// try the value form first (the current reality) and fall back to the pointer
349	// form. TestAsRefRejectedError_RealReportStatusPath drives go-git's real
350	// report.Error() so a deeper type change still fails loud in CI.
351	func commandStatusErr(err error) (packp.CommandStatusErr, bool) {
352		var byVal packp.CommandStatusErr
353		if errors.As(err, &byVal) {
354			return byVal, true
355		}
356		var byPtr *packp.CommandStatusErr
357		if errors.As(err, &byPtr) && byPtr != nil {
358			return *byPtr, true
359		}
360		return packp.CommandStatusErr{}, false
361	}
362	
363	// asRefRejectedError wraps a target receive-pack report-status "ng" error in
364	// a typed *RefRejectedError so callers can branch on errors.As /
365	// errors.Is(err, ErrTargetRefMoved) instead of substring-matching the free-form
366	// reason themselves. Inputs that are not a per-ref command status (e.g. an
367	// unpack-status error) pass through unchanged. The input is preserved via Unwrap,
368	// so the message and the underlying packp.CommandStatusErr stay reachable.
369	func asRefRejectedError(err error) error {
370		cs, ok := commandStatusErr(err)
371		if !ok {
372			return err
373		}
374		return &RefRejectedError{
375			Ref:    cs.ReferenceName.String(),
376			Reason: cs.Status,
377			moved:  isConcurrentMove(cs.Status),
378			err:    err,
379		}
380	}
381	
382	// sendReceivePack encodes and POSTs a receive-pack request, then decodes the report.
383	func sendReceivePack(
384		ctx context.Context,
385		conn Conn,
386		req *packp.UpdateRequests,
387		packData io.Reader,
388		verbose bool,
389		onRejection func(plumbing.ReferenceName, string),
390	) error {
391		var header bytes.Buffer
392		if err := req.Encode(&header); err != nil {
393			return fmt.Errorf("encode update-request: %w", err)
394		}
395		// The push body is io.MultiReader(header, packData); packData comes
396		// from a live upload-pack pipe and isn't rewindable, so a mid-stream
397		// 401 can't trigger PostRPCStreamBody's normal helper retry. Probe
398		// for auth requirements with a same-shape POST first.
399		if hc, ok := conn.(*HTTPConn); ok {
400			hc.EnsureAuthForService(ctx, transport.ReceivePackService)
401		}
402		body := io.Reader(bytes.NewReader(header.Bytes()))
403		if packData != nil {
404			body = io.MultiReader(body, packData)
405		}
406		reader, err := PostRPCStreamBody(ctx, conn, transport.ReceivePackService, body, false, "receive-pack push")
407		if err != nil {
408			return fmt.Errorf("target receive-pack: %w", err)
409		}
410		defer reader.Close()
411	
412		// Unwrap sideband if negotiated; stream server-side progress to stderr
413		// when verbose so long-running pushes show "Resolving deltas ..." etc.
414		var respReader io.Reader = reader
415		switch {
416		case req.Capabilities.Supports(capability.Sideband64k):
417			dem := sideband.NewDemuxer(sideband.Sideband64k, reader)
418			dem.Progress = progressSink(verbose, "target: ", conn.ProgressWriter())
419			respReader = dem
420		case req.Capabilities.Supports(capability.Sideband):
421			dem := sideband.NewDemuxer(sideband.Sideband, reader)
422			dem.Progress = progressSink(verbose, "target: ", conn.ProgressWriter())
423			respReader = dem
424		}
425	
426		if req.Capabilities.Supports(capability.ReportStatus) {
427			report := &packp.ReportStatus{}
428			if err := report.Decode(respReader); err != nil {
429				return fmt.Errorf("decode report-status: %w", err)
430			}
431			if onRejection == nil {
432				if err := report.Error(); err != nil {
433					return fmt.Errorf("report-status: %w", asRefRejectedError(annotateLeaseFailure(err)))
434				}
435				return nil
436			}
437			if report.UnpackStatus != "" && report.UnpackStatus != "ok" {
438				return fmt.Errorf("report-status: unpack error: %s", report.UnpackStatus)
439			}
440			for _, cs := range report.CommandStatuses {
441				if cs.Status == "" || cs.Status == "ok" {
442					continue
443				}
444				onRejection(cs.ReferenceName, cs.Status)
445			}
446		}
447		return nil
448	}
449	
450	// PushObjects pushes locally-materialized objects to the target.
451	//
452	// A push within the per-request ref-update limit (see effectiveMaxRefUpdates)
453	// is a single atomic receive-pack request. A larger push is split: the
454	// materialized pack — which carries every object for the whole push — rides
455	// with the first batch of object-bearing commands, then the remaining refs (and
456	// any deletes) move as ref-only updates because the objects are already
457	// committed.
458	func PushObjects(
459		ctx context.Context,
460		conn Conn,
461		adv *packp.AdvRefs,
462		commands []PushCommand,
463		store storer.Storer,
464		hashes []plumbing.Hash,
465		maxRefUpdates int,
466		verbose bool,
467		onRejection func(plumbing.ReferenceName, string),
468	) error {
469		limit := effectiveMaxRefUpdates(maxRefUpdates)
470		if len(commands) <= limit {
471			return pushObjectsBatch(ctx, conn, adv, commands, store, hashes, verbose, onRejection)
472		}
473	
474		updates := make([]PushCommand, 0, len(commands))
475		var deletes []PushCommand
476		for _, c := range commands {
477			if c.Delete {
478				deletes = append(deletes, c)
479			} else {
480				updates = append(updates, c)
481			}
482		}
483	
484		if len(updates) > 0 {
485			first, rest := splitFirstBatch(updates, limit)
486			if err := pushObjectsBatch(ctx, conn, adv, first, store, hashes, verbose, onRejection); err != nil {
487				return err
488			}
489			if len(rest) > 0 {
490				if err := PushCommands(ctx, conn, adv, rest, maxRefUpdates, verbose, onRejection); err != nil {
491					return err
492				}
493			}
494		}
495		if len(deletes) > 0 {
496			return PushCommands(ctx, conn, adv, deletes, maxRefUpdates, verbose, onRejection)
497		}
498		return nil
499	}
500	
501	// pushObjectsBatch encodes the selected objects into a pack and sends one
502	// receive-pack request for commands.
503	//
504	// Delta selection runs synchronously up front via
505	// packfile.DeltaSelector. The selected objects are then handed back to
506	// a packfile.Encoder behind a passthrough ObjectSelector, so the
507	// encoder's write phase (Encode → encode(objects)) streams pack bytes
508	// continuously into an io.Pipe to the HTTP request body. This avoids
509	// the mid-stream stall that occurs when Encode runs selection itself —
510	// CDN edges treat the resulting idle gap as a stalled upload and close
511	// the connection. See go-git PR #2142 for the API hook.
512	func pushObjectsBatch(
513		ctx context.Context,
514		conn Conn,
515		adv *packp.AdvRefs,
516		commands []PushCommand,
517		store storer.Storer,
518		hashes []plumbing.Hash,
519		verbose bool,
520		onRejection func(plumbing.ReferenceName, string),
521	) error {
522		req, _, hasUpdates, err := buildUpdateRequest(adv, commands, verbose)
523		if err != nil {
524			return err
525		}
526		if !hasUpdates {
527			return sendReceivePack(ctx, conn, req, nil, verbose, onRejection)
528		}
529	
530		progressDest := progressSink(verbose, "target: ", conn.ProgressWriter())
531	
532		stopSelect := startSelectionProgress(progressDest)
533		objects, err := packfile.NewDeltaSelector(store).ObjectsToPack(hashes, 10)
534		stopSelect(len(objects), err)
535		if err != nil {
536			return fmt.Errorf("select objects to pack: %w", err)
537		}
538	
539		useRefDeltas := !adv.Capabilities.Supports(capability.OFSDelta)
540		pr, pw := io.Pipe()
541		done := make(chan error, 1)
542		go func() {
543			cw := &countingWriter{w: pw}
544			stopWrite := startPackWriteProgress(cw, progressDest)
545			defer stopWrite()
546			enc := packfile.NewEncoder(cw, store, useRefDeltas,
547				packfile.WithObjectSelector(precomputedSelector{objects: objects}))
548			if _, err := enc.Encode(hashes, 10); err != nil {
549				done <- pw.CloseWithError(fmt.Errorf("encode packfile: %w", err))
550				return
551			}
552			done <- pw.Close()
553		}()
554	
555		err = sendReceivePack(ctx, conn, req, pr, verbose, onRejection)
556		_ = pr.Close()
557		encodeErr := <-done
558		if err != nil {
559			return err
560		}
561		return encodeErr
562	}
563	
564	// precomputedSelector is a packfile.ObjectSelector that returns a
565	// fixed []*packfile.ObjectToPack, ignoring its arguments. It is the
566	// passthrough used by PushObjects to feed pre-selected objects back
567	// into packfile.Encoder via WithObjectSelector. Used exactly once per
568	// PushObjects call and not exposed outside this package.
569	type precomputedSelector struct {
570		objects []*packfile.ObjectToPack
571	}
572	
573	func (p precomputedSelector) ObjectsToPack(_ []plumbing.Hash, _ uint) ([]*packfile.ObjectToPack, error) {
574		return p.objects, nil
575	}
576	
577	// countingWriter wraps an io.Writer and tracks total bytes written.
578	// The count is read by the progress ticker concurrently with the
579	// encoder's writes, so the counter is atomic.
580	type countingWriter struct {
581		w io.Writer
582		n atomic.Int64
583	}
584	
585	func (cw *countingWriter) Write(p []byte) (int, error) {
586		n, err := cw.w.Write(p)
587		cw.n.Add(int64(n))
588		if err != nil {
589			return n, fmt.Errorf("counting writer: %w", err)
590		}
591		return n, nil
592	}
593	
594	func (cw *countingWriter) Count() int64 { return cw.n.Load() }
595	
596	// startSelectionProgress emits in-place "selecting deltas, elapsed X"
597	// updates every 500ms during the synchronous delta-selection phase of
598	// PushObjects. The returned stop function takes the number of selected
599	// objects and the selection error (nil on success); on success it
600	// finalizes the line with a permanent "selected N objects in Y"
601	// summary, on error it just stops the ticker without claiming success.
602	// When dest is nil (non-verbose mode) returns a no-op stop, so
603	// callers don't need to special-case verbosity.
604	//
605	// Selection has no observable byte progress — go-git's DeltaSelector
606	// is opaque to the caller — so elapsed time is the only signal we can
607	// surface to keep long selections from looking like a hang.
608	func startSelectionProgress(dest io.Writer) func(objectCount int, err error) {
609		if dest == nil {
610			return func(int, error) {}
611		}
612		start := time.Now()
613		ticker := time.NewTicker(500 * time.Millisecond)
614		stop := make(chan struct{})
615		done := make(chan struct{})
616		go func() {
617			defer close(done)
618			for {
619				select {
620				case <-stop:
621					return
622				case <-ticker.C:
623					fmt.Fprintf(dest, "selecting deltas, elapsed %s\r",
624						time.Since(start).Round(time.Second))
625				}
626			}
627		}()
628		return func(objectCount int, err error) {
629			ticker.Stop()
630			close(stop)
631			<-done
632			if err != nil {
633				return
634			}
635			fmt.Fprintf(dest, "selected %d objects in %s\n",
636				objectCount, time.Since(start).Round(time.Second))
637		}
638	}
639	
640	// startPackWriteProgress emits in-place "encoding pack: N MB, elapsed
641	// X" updates every 500ms while the encoder writes pack bytes through
642	// cw. The returned stop function finalizes the line with a permanent
643	// "encoded pack" summary. Single-use, typically via defer. When dest
644	// is nil returns a no-op stop.
645	//
646	// This is the second of two phases visible to a materialized push:
647	// startSelectionProgress runs synchronously first, then
648	// startPackWriteProgress takes over once selection has completed and
649	// the encoder begins streaming bytes to the request body.
650	func startPackWriteProgress(cw *countingWriter, dest io.Writer) func() {
651		if dest == nil {
652			return func() {}
653		}
654		start := time.Now()
655		ticker := time.NewTicker(500 * time.Millisecond)
656		stop := make(chan struct{})
657		done := make(chan struct{})
658		go func() {
659			defer close(done)
660			for {
661				select {
662				case <-stop:
663					return
664				case <-ticker.C:
665					fmt.Fprintf(dest, "encoding pack: %s, elapsed %s\r",
666						humanizeBytes(cw.Count()), time.Since(start).Round(time.Second))
667				}
668			}
669		}()
670		return func() {
671			ticker.Stop()
672			close(stop)
673			<-done
674			fmt.Fprintf(dest, "encoded pack: %s in %s\n",
675				humanizeBytes(cw.Count()), time.Since(start).Round(time.Second))
676		}
677	}
678	
679	// humanizeBytes renders n in IEC units with one decimal place for KB+
680	// (e.g. "47.3 MB"). Anything below 1 KB is shown as raw bytes.
681	func humanizeBytes(n int64) string {
682		const (
683			kb = 1024
684			mb = kb * 1024
685			gb = mb * 1024
686		)
687		switch {
688		case n < kb:
689			return fmt.Sprintf("%d B", n)
690		case n < mb:
691			return fmt.Sprintf("%.1f KB", float64(n)/float64(kb))
692		case n < gb:
693			return fmt.Sprintf("%.1f MB", float64(n)/float64(mb))
694		default:
695			return fmt.Sprintf("%.1f GB", float64(n)/float64(gb))
696		}
697	}
698	
699	// PushPack pushes a pack stream (relay) to the target.
700	func PushPack(
701		ctx context.Context,
702		conn Conn,
703		adv *packp.AdvRefs,
704		commands []PushCommand,
705		pack io.ReadCloser,
706		maxRefUpdates int,
707		verbose bool,
708		onRejection func(plumbing.ReferenceName, string),
709	) error {
710		for _, cmd := range commands {
711			if cmd.Delete {
712				_ = pack.Close()
713				return errors.New("pack push only supports create and update actions")
714			}
715		}
716	
717		// The pack carries every object for all commands, so it rides with the
718		// first batch; once committed the remaining refs update without re-sending
719		// objects. This keeps each request under the target's per-push ref-update
720		// limit (see effectiveMaxRefUpdates).
721		first, rest := splitFirstBatch(commands, effectiveMaxRefUpdates(maxRefUpdates))
722	
723		req, _, _, err := buildUpdateRequest(adv, first, verbose)
724		if err != nil {
725			_ = pack.Close()
726			return err
727		}
728	
729		err = sendReceivePack(ctx, conn, req, pack, verbose, onRejection)
730		closeErr := pack.Close()
731		if err != nil {
732			return err
733		}
734		if closeErr != nil {
735			return fmt.Errorf("close pack: %w", closeErr)
736		}
737	
738		if len(rest) > 0 {
739			return PushCommands(ctx, conn, adv, rest, maxRefUpdates, verbose, onRejection)
740		}
741		return nil
742	}
743	
744	// PushCommands sends ref update commands that move no new objects to the
745	// target — the referenced objects already exist there.
746	//
747	// A create/update command still carries a valid empty pack (12-byte header,
748	// zero objects, trailing checksum). Pack-less creates are legal git, but some
749	// receive-pack implementations read a pack header for every non-delete command
750	// and fail with a truncated-pack error when the request body ends after the
751	// commands; an explicit empty pack satisfies them and stays valid for servers
752	// that tolerate the pack-less form. Delete-only pushes carry no pack, as git
753	// requires.
754	func PushCommands(
755		ctx context.Context,
756		conn Conn,
757		adv *packp.AdvRefs,
758		commands []PushCommand,
759		maxRefUpdates int,
760		verbose bool,
761		onRejection func(plumbing.ReferenceName, string),
762	) error {
763		batches := chunkRefUpdates(commands, effectiveMaxRefUpdates(maxRefUpdates))
764		for i, batch := range batches {
765			// Ref-only batches carry no useful target progress; suppress the empty
766			// sideband (verbose=false) and report completion ourselves so a large
767			// push doesn't spew a bare "target:" line per batch.
768			if err := pushCommandsBatch(ctx, conn, adv, batch, false, onRejection); err != nil {
769				return err
770			}
771			logRefUpdateBatch(conn, verbose, i+1, len(batches), len(batch))
772		}
773		return nil
774	}
775	
776	// pushCommandsBatch sends one receive-pack request for a single batch of
777	// ref-only commands; the referenced objects must already exist on the target.
778	func pushCommandsBatch(
779		ctx context.Context,
780		conn Conn,
781		adv *packp.AdvRefs,
782		commands []PushCommand,
783		verbose bool,
784		onRejection func(plumbing.ReferenceName, string),
785	) error {
786		req, _, hasUpdates, err := buildUpdateRequest(adv, commands, verbose)
787		if err != nil {
788			return err
789		}
790		var packData io.Reader
791		if hasUpdates {
792			packData = bytes.NewReader(emptyPack(adv))
793		}
794		return sendReceivePack(ctx, conn, req, packData, verbose, onRejection)
795	}
796	
797	// emptyPackHeader is the fixed 12-byte prefix of any packfile with zero
798	// objects: the "PACK" signature, version 2, and an object count of 0.
799	var emptyPackHeader = []byte{'P', 'A', 'C', 'K', 0, 0, 0, 2, 0, 0, 0, 0}
800	
801	// A valid empty pack is emptyPackHeader followed by the trailing checksum over
802	// it. The bytes depend only on the hash algorithm, so the two possibilities are
803	// computed once at package load rather than on every PushCommands call.
804	var (
805		emptyPackSHA1   = buildEmptyPack(crypto.SHA1)
806		emptyPackSHA256 = buildEmptyPack(crypto.SHA256)
807	)
808	
809	func buildEmptyPack(algo crypto.Hash) []byte {
810		h := hash.New(algo)
811		_, _ = h.Write(emptyPackHeader)
812		return append(slices.Clone(emptyPackHeader), h.Sum(nil)...)
813	}
814	
815	// emptyPack returns a valid packfile containing zero objects whose trailing
816	// checksum matches the target's advertised object format: SHA-256 repositories
817	// get a 32-byte trailer; everything else uses the 20-byte SHA-1 trailer.
818	func emptyPack(adv *packp.AdvRefs) []byte {
819		if vals := adv.Capabilities.Get(capability.ObjectFormat); len(vals) > 0 && vals[0] == "sha256" {
820			return emptyPackSHA256
821		}
822		return emptyPackSHA1
823	}
824	
825	func progressWriter(verbose bool, dest io.Writer) io.Writer {
826		if !verbose {
827			return nil
828		}
829		if dest == nil {
830			dest = os.Stderr
831		}
832		return dest
833	}
834	
835	// progressSink returns a line-prefixing io.Writer suitable for
836	// sideband.Demuxer.Progress. When verbose is false it returns nil so the
837	// demuxer discards progress frames without allocating. Passing a non-nil
838	// dest routes the prefixed lines through that writer instead of os.Stderr,
839	// which lets a live progress reporter coordinate output.
840	func progressSink(verbose bool, prefix string, dest io.Writer) io.Writer {
841		if !verbose {
842			return nil
843		}
844		if dest == nil {
845			dest = os.Stderr
846		}
847		return &prefixedLineWriter{w: dest, prefix: prefix, atLineStart: true}
848	}
849	
850	// prefixedLineWriter prepends a fixed prefix to each line of input written
851	// to the wrapped writer. Git sideband progress arrives as chunks that may
852	// contain '\n' between full lines or '\r' for in-place updates ("Resolving
853	// deltas:  12%\r"); both are treated as line terminators so the next chunk
854	// gets a fresh prefix.
855	type prefixedLineWriter struct {
856		w           io.Writer
857		prefix      string
858		atLineStart bool
859	}
860	
861	func (p *prefixedLineWriter) Write(b []byte) (int, error) {
862		consumed := 0
863		for len(b) > 0 {
864			if p.atLineStart {
865				if _, err := io.WriteString(p.w, p.prefix); err != nil {
866					return consumed, fmt.Errorf("write prefix: %w", err)
867				}
868				p.atLineStart = false
869			}
870			i := bytes.IndexAny(b, "\r\n")
871			var chunk []byte
872			if i < 0 {
873				chunk = b
874			} else {
875				chunk = b[:i+1]
876				p.atLineStart = true
877			}
878			n, err := p.w.Write(chunk)
879			consumed += n
880			if err != nil {
881				return consumed, fmt.Errorf("write chunk: %w", err)
882			}
883			b = b[len(chunk):]
884		}
885		return consumed, nil
886	}

content.text: The push path drives `PostRPCStreamBody(..., ReceivePackService, ..., v2=false)` and gets the advertisement via `RequestInfoRefs(..., receive-pack, "")`. The gating risk is whether `git-remote-entire` actually supports `stateless-connect` for **receive-pack** (push) — the spec says stateless-connect is v2/fetch-oriented, and the helper also advertised the legacy `push` capability. If push doesn't work over stateless-connect, the whole approach changes. Let me probe both services empirically before building anything.

content.text:
Critical detail confirmed for both:
- **upload-pack (v2)**: ack `\n`, then `version 2\n` + caps + flush `0000`, then idle waiting for a request.
- **receive-pack (v0)**: ack `\n`, then `…capabilities^{}\x00 report-status delete-refs…` + flush `0000`. So push over stateless-connect IS implemented by the helper.

The one thing I'm not certain about is the receive-pack **request** framing over stateless-connect (the raw packfile gets appended after the command flush — does the helper forward it correctly?). The authority on that contract is git itself, since `git push entire://` drives this same helper. Let me find the helper source to confirm rather than guess.

content.text: Key finding: `handleStatelessConnect` treats the two services **differently**. upload-pack gets full v2 stateless framing (request→POST→response + `0002`). But **receive-pack falls through to `handleConnect`** — a raw bidirectional proxy, no `0002`. Let me read `handleConnect` to nail the push framing.

aiTitle: Fix unsupported protocol scheme in git-sync replicate

content.text:
Now the architecture is clear and the framing differs by service:

- **upload-pack (fetch)**: helper uses the v2 **stateless** path — ack `\n`, advertisement (`version 2…0000`, no HTTP banner), then per-request POST with a trailing `0002` response-end. git-sync fetches with v2, so this is the clean path.
- **receive-pack (push)**: helper falls through to `handleConnect` — a raw proxy. It streams the **banner-stripped** v0 advertisement, then `ReadReceivePackRequest` reads the whole request (commands+pack) from stdin, POSTs it, streams `report-status` back. No `0002`.

So the helper conn must read advertisements/responses differently per service. I need to confirm exactly how the bytes look vs what git-sync's decoders expect (banner handling, response termination). Let me read git-sync's decoders and the helper's request readers together.

content.text:
Now I have the full contract. Decisive facts:

- **receive-pack request** (`ReadReceivePackRequest`): reads commands until flush, then if any non-delete command, `io.Copy` the pack **until EOF**. So git-sync must **close the helper's stdin** after writing the push body → **one receive-pack RPC per helper process**.
- **v2 upload-pack** (`ReadFlushTerminatedMessage`): reads one flush-terminated request, POSTs, appends `0002`. Stateless, so a fresh process per RPC is safe too.
- Ack is exactly `\n`; `fallback`/error otherwise.

Let me confirm the helper's command loop (do I need to send `capabilities`/`option` first, or is `stateless-connect` accepted directly — my probe suggests yes) by reading `run.go`.

content.content:
1	package githelper
2	
3	import (
4		"bufio"
5		"context"
6		"errors"
7		"fmt"
8		"io"
9		"strings"
10	
11		"github.com/entireio/cli/internal/remotehelper/debuglog"
12	)
13	
14	// Run drives the git-remote-helper protocol loop against the given
15	// Transport. It reads commands from stdin one line at a time and
16	// writes responses to stdout until git closes the pipe or sends an
17	// unsupported command (which terminates the loop with an error).
18	//
19	// protocolVersion selects which capability set to advertise. v2 (the
20	// upstream default since Git 2.26) advertises `stateless-connect` +
21	// `push` so Git negotiates wire-protocol v2 over framed HTTP RPC.
22	// v0/v1 advertises `connect`, giving Git a raw pipe to speak the
23	// legacy smart-HTTP protocol through. Upstream Git's
24	// transport-helper.c always prefers `connect` over `stateless-connect`
25	// when both are present, so the advertised set has to match the
26	// resolved protocol.version rather than offering both.
27	//
28	// The Transport interface decouples this loop from any specific HTTP
29	// implementation — see the entire/transport package for the
30	// production wiring.
31	func Run(ctx context.Context, t Transport, protocolVersion int, stdin io.Reader, stdout io.Writer) error {
32		commandReader := bufio.NewReader(stdin)
33		opts := &Options{}
34	
35		for {
36			line, err := commandReader.ReadString('\n')
37			if err != nil {
38				if errors.Is(err, io.EOF) && line == "" {
39					return nil
40				}
41				if !errors.Is(err, io.EOF) {
42					return fmt.Errorf("reading protocol input: %w", err)
43				}
44			}
45			line = strings.TrimSuffix(line, "\n")
46			line = strings.TrimSuffix(line, "\r")
47			debuglog.Printf("command: %q", line)
48	
49			switch {
50			case line == "capabilities":
51				if protocolVersion >= 2 {
52					fmt.Fprintln(stdout, "stateless-connect")
53					fmt.Fprintln(stdout, "push")
54				} else {
55					fmt.Fprintln(stdout, "connect")
56				}
57				fmt.Fprintln(stdout, "option")
58				fmt.Fprintln(stdout)
59	
60			case line == "list" || line == "list for-push":
61				if err := handleList(ctx, t, line == "list for-push", stdout); err != nil {
62					return err
63				}
64	
65			case strings.HasPrefix(line, "option "):
66				name, value, _ := strings.Cut(strings.TrimPrefix(line, "option "), " ")
67				fmt.Fprintln(stdout, opts.Set(name, value))
68	
69			case strings.HasPrefix(line, "stateless-connect "):
70				service := strings.TrimPrefix(line, "stateless-connect ")
71				if err := handleStatelessConnect(ctx, t, service, commandReader, stdout); err != nil {
72					return err
73				}
74				return nil
75	
76			case strings.HasPrefix(line, "connect "):
77				service := strings.TrimPrefix(line, "connect ")
78				if service != serviceUploadPack && service != serviceReceivePack {
79					return fmt.Errorf("unsupported service: %s", service)
80				}
81				fmt.Fprintln(stdout)
82				if err := handleConnect(ctx, t, service, commandReader, stdout); err != nil {
83					return err
84				}
85				return nil
86	
87			case strings.HasPrefix(line, "push "):
88				if err := handlePush(ctx, t, line, opts, commandReader, stdout); err != nil {
89					return err
90				}
91	
92			case line == "":
93				if errors.Is(err, io.EOF) {
94					return nil
95				}
96				continue
97	
98			default:
99				return fmt.Errorf("unsupported command: %s", line)
100			}
101		}
102	}
103

content.text:
Complete protocol picture now. The helper accepts `stateless-connect <service>` directly, and after the handler returns the process exits — so it's **one stateless-connect session per process**. For v2 upload-pack the session services multiple framed requests (reuse the process); for receive-pack `handleConnect` is one-shot and reads the pack until stdin EOF (fresh process per push batch).

47	func main() {
48		os.Exit(run(os.Args))
49	}
50	
51	func run(args []string) int {
52		// --version / --help only activate as the sole argument (so os.Args has
53		// length 2). Git always invokes the helper as
54		// `git-remote-entire <remote-name> <url>` (os.Args length 3), so these can
55		// never collide with a real remote-helper invocation.
56		if len(args) == 2 {
57			if text, ok := infoFlagText(args[1], loadedVersion()); ok {
58				fmt.Fprint(os.Stdout, text)
59				return 0
60			}
61		}
62	
63		if len(args) < 3 {
64			fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, "usage: %s <remote-name> <url>\n", remotehelper.BinaryName)
65			return 128
66		}
67	
68		// Build info drives the identifier the helper advertises upstream.
69		// One string covers both surfaces:
70		//   - githelper.Agent rides in the git protocol pkt-line agent=
71		//     capability appended to upload-pack / receive-pack / v2 requests.
72		//   - httpUserAgent rides in the HTTP User-Agent header on every
73		//     outbound request so server access logs can attribute traffic.
74		// Using the same value keeps the two log surfaces correlatable.
75		versioninfo.Load()
76		helperAgent := remotehelper.BinaryName + "/" + versioninfo.Version
77		githelper.Agent = helperAgent
78		httpUserAgent := helperAgent
79	
80		rawURL := args[2]
81		parsedURL, err := url.Parse(rawURL)
82		switch {
83		case err != nil:
84			fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, "fatal: invalid URL %q: %v\n", rawURL, err)
85			return 128
86		case parsedURL.Scheme != "entire":
87			fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, "fatal: unsupported URL scheme %q (expected 'entire')\n", parsedURL.Scheme)
88			return 128
89		case parsedURL.Host == "":
90			fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, "fatal: missing host in URL %q\n", rawURL)
91			return 128
92		}
93	
94		ctx, stop := installSignals()
95		defer stop()
96	
97		skipTLS := os.Getenv("ENTIRE_TLS_SKIP_VERIFY") == "true"
98	
99		nodeCfg := replicas.Resolve(parsedURL)
100		// The repo-scoped token's audience is <clusterBaseURL><repoSlug>. The
101		// audience pins to the cluster entry URL (not a replica node), matching
102		// what the server validates the exchanged token against.
103		clusterBaseURL := nodeCfg.EntryURL
104		repoSlug := parsedURL.Path
105	
106		httpClient := &http.Client{
107			Timeout: 30 * time.Second,
108			Transport: &httpclient.UserAgentTransport{
109				Next: httpclient.NewTransport(skipTLS),
110				UA:   httpUserAgent,
111			},
112		}
113	
114		creds, err := resolveCreds(ctx, parsedURL, clusterBaseURL, skipTLS, httpClient)
115		if err != nil {
116			fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, "fatal: %v\n", err)
117			return 128
118		}
119	
120		setAuth := func(req *http.Request) error {
121			action := gitActionFromRequest(req)
122			if action == "" {
123				return fmt.Errorf("cannot classify git op for %s %s; scoped-token exchange requires a recognised smart-HTTP endpoint", req.Method, req.URL.Path)
124			}
125			token, err := creds.Token(req.Context(), repoSlug, action)
126			if err != nil {
127				return fmt.Errorf("repo-scoped token exchange: %w", err)
128			}
129			req.Header.Set("Authorization", "Bearer "+token)
130			return nil
131		}
132	
133		var onNodeFailed func(string)
134		if nodeCfg.Caching() {
135			onNodeFailed = func(string) { replicas.Invalidate(nodeCfg.ClusterHost, nodeCfg.RepoPath) }
136		}
137	
138		proxy := transport.New(transport.Config{
139			Nodes:        nodeCfg,
140			Path:         parsedURL.Path,
141			SkipTLS:      skipTLS,
142			SetAuth:      setAuth,
143			OnNodeFailed: onNodeFailed,
144			UserAgent:    httpUserAgent,
145		})
146	
147		protocolVersion := resolveProtocolVersion()
148		debuglog.Printf("git protocol.version=%d (v2 advertises stateless-connect + push; v0/v1 advertises connect)", protocolVersion)
149	
150		if err := githelper.Run(ctx, proxy, protocolVersion, os.Stdin, os.Stdout); err != nil {
151			fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, "fatal: %v\n", err)
152			return 128
153		}
154		return 0
155	}
156	
157	// loadedVersion populates the build info and returns the resolved version.
158	func loadedVersion() string {
159		versioninfo.Load()
160		return versioninfo.Version
161	}
162	
163	// infoFlagText renders the output for the standalone --version / --help flags,
164	// returning false for anything else. Kept pure (version passed in, no globals)
165	// so it's unit-testable.
166	func infoFlagText(flag, version string) (string, bool) {
167		switch flag {
168		case "--version":
169			return fmt.Sprintf("%s %s\nGo version: %s\nOS/Arch: %s/%s\n",
170				remotehelper.BinaryName, version, runtime.Version(), runtime.GOOS, runtime.GOARCH), true
171		case "--help":
172			return fmt.Sprintf("%s %s\n\n"+
173				"This is a helper which Git calls when encountering entire://... URLs.  "+
174				"For more information see https://github.com/entireio/cli.\n",
175				remotehelper.BinaryName, version), true
176		}
177		return "", false
178	}
179

181	// the GIT_PROTOCOL environment variable. The value is a colon-

183	// 0, 1, or 2; any other value emits a stderr warning and falls
184	// back to 2 — upstream Git's default since 2.26.

attachment.snippet:
1	package gitproto
2	
3	import (
4		"bufio"
5		"bytes"
6		"context"
7		"errors"
8		"fmt"
9		"io"
10		"net/url"
11		"os"
12		"os/exec"
13		"strings"
14		"sync"
15	)
16	
17	// RemoteHelperLookPath resolves the git remote-helper binary for a URL scheme,
18	// following git's `git-remote-<scheme>` naming convention. Replaceable in
19	// tests; production wiring just calls exec.LookPath.
20	var RemoteHelperLookPath = func(scheme string) (string, bool) {
21		path, err := exec.LookPath("git-remote-" + scheme)
22		if err != nil {
23			return "", false
24		}
25		return path, true
26	}
27	
28	// LookupRemoteHelper reports whether a git remote helper is installed for the
29	// given URL scheme (e.g. "entire" → git-remote-entire). Schemes git-sync
30	// speaks natively (http/https/ssh) should never be routed here — git ships
31	// git-remote-http(s), and diverting to it would bypass the optimized native
32	// transport.
33	func LookupRemoteHelper(scheme string) (string, bool) {
34		if scheme == "" {
35			return "", false
36		}
37		return RemoteHelperLookPath(scheme)
38	}
39	
40	// HelperConn speaks the git remote-helper protocol (gitremote-helpers(7)) to a
41	// git-remote-<scheme> binary, bridging git-sync's smart-transport Conn onto the
42	// helper's stateless-connect capability. It lets git-sync drive schemes it has
43	// no native transport for (e.g. entire://) by delegating auth and the actual
44	// network I/O to the helper, while still running the wire protocol itself.
45	//
46	// Each Conn operation spawns its own helper process and tears it down when the
47	// operation completes. This is deliberate, not lazy: the helper services
48	// exactly one stateless-connect session per process (its protocol loop returns
49	// once the session ends), and its receive-pack path reads the pushed pack until
50	// stdin EOF — both of which make "fresh process per RPC" the simplest correct
51	// model, and it naturally supports git-sync's batched (multi-request) pushes.
52	// The cost is one extra info/refs request per RPC, negligible beside pack
53	// transfer.
54	//
55	// Like the SSH transport, the helper bridge has no per-request byte accounting,
56	// so --stats omits helper-side throughput.
57	type HelperConn struct {
58		HelperPath  string
59		EndpointURL *url.URL
60		// RawURL is the user-typed URL (e.g. entire://host/path), passed to the
61		// helper verbatim — the helper, not git-sync, owns scheme interpretation.
62		RawURL      string
63		Label       string
64		progressOut io.Writer
65	}
66	
67	// NewHelperConn builds a remote-helper-backed connection. helperPath is the
68	// resolved git-remote-<scheme> binary; rawURL is the URL as the user typed it.
69	func NewHelperConn(helperPath string, ep *url.URL, rawURL, label string) *HelperConn {
70		return &HelperConn{HelperPath: helperPath, EndpointURL: ep, RawURL: rawURL, Label: label}
71	}
72	
73	func (c *HelperConn) Endpoint() *url.URL { return c.EndpointURL }
74	
75	func (c *HelperConn) ProgressWriter() io.Writer { return c.progressOut }
76	
77	func (c *HelperConn) SetProgressWriter(w io.Writer) { c.progressOut = w }
78	
79	// Close is a no-op: HelperConn owns no long-lived process. Each RPC manages its
80	// own helper process lifetime.
81	func (c *HelperConn) Close() error { return nil }
82	
83	// RequestInfoRefs spawns the helper, opens a stateless-connect session for the
84	// service, and returns the advertisement the helper emits (the v2 capability
85	// advertisement for upload-pack, or the v0 ref advertisement for receive-pack).
86	// The helper strips the smart-HTTP "# service=" banner itself, so the bytes
87	// returned match what the SSH transport produces — bannerless and terminated by
88	// a flush, which both DecodeV2Capabilities and decodeV1AdvRefs already accept.
89	func (c *HelperConn) RequestInfoRefs(ctx context.Context, service string, gitProtocol string) ([]byte, error) {
90		_ = gitProtocol // the helper negotiates v2 with the remote itself
91		proc, err := c.dial(ctx, service)
92		if err != nil {
93			return nil, err
94		}
95		adv, readErr := readAdvertisement(proc.out)
96		finishErr := proc.finish()
97		if readErr != nil {
98			return nil, fmt.Errorf("%s advertisement: %w", service, errors.Join(readErr, finishErr))
99		}
100		if finishErr != nil {
101			return nil, fmt.Errorf("%s advertisement: %w", service, finishErr)
102		}
103		return adv, nil
104	}
105	
106	// PostRPCStreamBody spawns the helper, opens a stateless-connect session, sends
107	// the request body, and returns the response stream. The helper always emits
108	// ack + advertisement before reading the request, so the advertisement is
109	// consumed and discarded first. For v2 the helper frames each response with a
110	// trailing response-end (0002) packet, which is stripped so the consumer sees
111	// exactly the byte stream it would over HTTP; for v0 (receive-pack) the response
112	// runs to EOF.
113	func (c *HelperConn) PostRPCStreamBody(ctx context.Context, service string, body io.Reader, v2 bool, phase string) (io.ReadCloser, error) {
114		_ = phase // helper transport has no per-RPC byte-stats tagging (like SSH)
115		proc, err := c.dial(ctx, service)
116		if err != nil {
117			return nil, err
118		}
119		if _, err := readAdvertisement(proc.out); err != nil {
120			return nil, fmt.Errorf("%s advertisement: %w", service, errors.Join(err, proc.cleanup()))
121		}
122	
123		copyErr := make(chan error, 1)
124		go func() {
125			_, err := io.Copy(proc.stdin, body)
126			// Closing stdin terminates the request: it bounds the v2
127			// flush-terminated read and, crucially, signals end-of-pack to the
128			// receive-pack handler, which copies the pack until EOF.
129			closeErr := proc.stdin.Close()
130			if err != nil {
131				copyErr <- err
132				return
133			}
134			copyErr <- closeErr
135		}()
136	
137		var resp io.Reader = proc.out
138		if v2 {
139			resp = newResponseEndReader(proc.out)
140		}
141		return &helperRPCStream{ctx: ctx, resp: resp, proc: proc, copyErr: copyErr}, nil
142	}
143	
144	// dial starts the helper process and opens a stateless-connect session for the
145	// service, consuming the helper's single-line acknowledgement. On success the
146	// helper is positioned to emit its advertisement.
147	func (c *HelperConn) dial(ctx context.Context, service string) (*helperProcess, error) {
148		// git invokes a remote helper as `git-remote-<scheme> <remote> <url>`.
149		// Passing the user-typed URL for both arguments matches git's handling of
150		// an anonymous (URL-only) remote, which is what git-sync always has.
151		cmd := exec.CommandContext(ctx, c.HelperPath, c.RawURL, c.RawURL)
152		// GIT_PROTOCOL=version=2 makes the helper advertise stateless-connect; we
153		// drive stateless-connect directly regardless, but this keeps the helper's
154		// own negotiation aligned with how we use it.
155		cmd.Env = append(os.Environ(), "GIT_PROTOCOL="+GitProtocolV2)
156		stderr := &sshCommandError{}
157		cmd.Stderr = stderr
158		stdout, err := cmd.StdoutPipe()
159		if err != nil {
160			return nil, fmt.Errorf("open helper stdout: %w", err)
161		}
162		stdin, err := cmd.StdinPipe()
163		if err != nil {
164			return nil, fmt.Errorf("open helper stdin: %w", err)
165		}
166		if err := cmd.Start(); err != nil {
167			return nil, fmt.Errorf("start remote helper %s: %w", c.HelperPath, stderr.wrap(err))
168		}
169		proc := &helperProcess{
170			cmd:    cmd,
171			stdin:  stdin,
172			stdout: stdout,
173			out:    bufio.NewReaderSize(stdout, 65536),
174			stderr: stderr,
175		}
176		if _, err := io.WriteString(stdin, "stateless-connect "+service+"\n"); err != nil {
177			return nil, fmt.Errorf("request stateless-connect %s: %w", service, errors.Join(err, proc.cleanup()))
178		}
179		if err := proc.readAck(); err != nil {
180			return nil, fmt.Errorf("stateless-connect %s: %w", service, errors.Join(err, proc.cleanup()))
181		}
182		return proc, nil
183	}
184	
185	// helperProcess wraps a single helper invocation and its pipes.
186	type helperProcess struct {
187		cmd    *exec.Cmd
188		stdin  io.WriteCloser
189		stdout io.ReadCloser
190		out    *bufio.Reader
191		stderr *sshCommandError
192

file.content:
### SSH remotes

`git-sync` also supports SSH remotes. Accepted forms include:

- `ssh://git@example.com/org/repo.git`
- `git@example.com:org/repo.git`
- `git+ssh://example.com/org/repo.git`

SSH transport shells out to the local `ssh` binary, so host aliases,
`IdentityFile`, agent-backed keys, and other `~/.ssh/config` behavior come
from your existing SSH setup rather than separate `git-sync` flags.

`git-sync` runs SSH with `BatchMode=yes`, which avoids interactive password or
host-key prompts during syncs. On first contact with a host, add it to
`known_hosts` ahead of time or configure `StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new`
for that host in your SSH config.

Current limitation: `--progress` and `--show-stats` do not yet include
byte-counted SSH transfer metrics, so `--progress` and `--stats` omit
SSH-side throughput.

If `ssh` is not available on `PATH`, `git-sync` fails early with a clear
`locate ssh binary` error before contacting either remote.

## Sync Behavior

input.old_string:
If `ssh` is not available on `PATH`, `git-sync` fails early with a clear
`locate ssh binary` error before contacting either remote.

## Sync Behavior
input.new_string:
If `ssh` is not available on `PATH`, `git-sync` fails early with a clear
`locate ssh binary` error before contacting either remote.

### Remote-helper schemes

For any URL scheme `git-sync` has no native transport for, it looks for a git
remote helper named `git-remote-<scheme>` on `PATH` — the same mechanism `git`
itself uses for custom schemes. If one is installed, `git-sync` delegates auth
and the network round trips to it while still driving the smart protocol over
the helper's `stateless-connect` bridge.

The motivating case is Entire's own `entire://` URLs:

```bash
git-sync replicate --all-refs \
  https://github.com/source-org/source-repo.git \
  entire://cluster-host/et/project/repo
```

This requires `git-remote-entire` (installed by the Entire CLI) on `PATH`.
Authentication — including the Entire context model and OAuth token refresh —
is handled entirely by the helper, so no `--target-token` is needed when you're
already logged in via the CLI. Both fetch (upload-pack, protocol v2) and push
(receive-pack) work through the helper.

`http`/`https`/`ssh` are never routed to a helper: `git-sync` always uses its
own optimized transports for those, even though `git` ships
`git-remote-http(s)`.

Like SSH, the helper bridge has no per-request byte accounting, so `--stats`
omits helper-side throughput (the other side's throughput still prints).

## Sync Behavior

It depends on repository size, network speed, and whether the relay path is available. As a rule of thumb, the relay path is bounded by source pack generation + network transfer + target receive-pack time — roughly the time of a `git clone` from the source plus a `git push` of the same pack. The materialized fallback adds local memory work for objects that need inspection.

For concrete numbers on your own setup, run the included benchmark tool against a representative repo; see [docs/testing.md](docs/testing.md#benchmarking).

### Does it support SSH?

Yes. `git-sync` supports SSH remotes through the local `ssh` binary, including
`ssh://`, SCP-style `git@host:path.git`, and `git+ssh://` URLs. See
[docs/usage.md](docs/usage.md) for details and current caveats.

### Does it run as a daemon or watch for changes?

No. `git-sync` is a one-shot CLI/library operation. To sync on a schedule or in response to events, run it from cron, CI, a worker, or another service.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
![git-sync theme](docs/images/gh-repo-cover.png "git-sync cover image")

# git-sync

`git-sync` mirrors refs from a source remote to a target remote without creating a local checkout. It uses an in-memory `go-git` object store and talks smart HTTP directly:

- `info/refs` ref advertisement for source and target
- `upload-pack` fetch from source with target tip hashes advertised as `have`
- `receive-pack` push to target with explicit ref update commands and a streamed packfile

That keeps the target side incremental without fetching target objects into the local process first.

## Why This Exists

Mirroring Git data between remotes usually means a local mirror clone followed by a mirror push. That's fine for small repos but turns a remote-to-remote operation into a local storage problem at scale, and shell glue around `git fetch` / `git push` tends to skip planning and structured output.

`git-sync` fills that gap. It streams source packs directly into target `receive-pack` when it can, plans every action before pushing, and emits typed JSON for automation.

For when to use it (and when not), see [docs/architecture.md](docs/architecture.md).

## Commands

The main commands are:

- `git-sync sync`: mirror source refs into the target
- `git-sync replicate`: overwrite target refs to match source via relay, and fail rather than materialize locally

`sync` automatically bootstraps an empty target, so the same command covers initial seeding and ongoing sync. To preview what would happen without pushing, run `git-sync plan` — it takes the same flags as `sync`, and `--mode replicate` previews a `replicate` run.

For one-off SHA1 → SHA256 repo conversion, `git-sync convert-sha256` fetches from an HTTP source and writes a new SHA256 bare repo on disk, with optional commit-message hash rewrites, an origin-notes ref, and a sidecar mapping file. See [docs/convert-sha256.md](docs/convert-sha256.md).

For command examples, JSON output, auth, protocol flags, and advanced command notes, see [docs/usage.md](docs/usage.md).

## Library API

`git-sync` is also a Go library. Use `entire.io/entire/git-sync` for the stable embedding surface (`Probe`, `Plan`, `Sync`, `Replicate`, typed results, auth and HTTP injection). `entire.io/entire/git-sync/unstable` exposes advanced controls (`Bootstrap`, `Fetch`, batching knobs, heap measurement) and is not stable.

## Installation

### Homebrew (macOS, Linux)

```bash
brew tap entireio/tap
brew install --cask git-sync
```

### `go install`

Requires Go 1.26 or newer.

```bash
go install entire.io/entire/git-sync/cmd/git-sync@latest
```

This drops a `git-sync` binary into `$(go env GOPATH)/bin`. Make sure that directory is on your `PATH`.

### From source

```bash
git clone https://github.com/entireio/git-sync.git
cd git-sync
go build -o git-sync ./cmd/git-sync
```

## Quick Start

```bash
git-sync sync \
  --source-token "$GITSYNC_SOURCE_TOKEN" \
  --target-token "$GITSYNC_TARGET_TOKEN" \
  https://github.com/source-org/source-repo.git \
  https://github.com/target-org/target-repo.git
```

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/60adb873-4032-4ab7-b236-24d038e04681

## Sync Behavior

`sync` picks the bootstrap relay path automatically when the target is empty. For non-empty targets, safe fast-forward updates also use a relay path that streams the source pack directly into target `receive-pack` without local materialization. Anything not relay-eligible (force, prune, deletes, tag retargets) falls back to a materialized path bounded by `--materialized-max-objects`.

For branch filtering, ref mapping, tags, pruning, protocol selection, JSON output, and auth details, see [docs/usage.md](docs/usage.md).

## Testing

Default suite:

```bash
env GOCACHE=/tmp/go-build go test ./...
```

Extended and environment-specific test instructions are in [docs/testing.md](docs/testing.md).

## Documentation

- [docs/usage.md](docs/usage.md) — CLI commands, examples, sync behavior, JSON output, auth, protocol notes
- [docs/architecture.md](docs/architecture.md) — product rationale, package layout, operation modes vs transfer modes, memory model
- [docs/protocol.md](docs/protocol.md) — smart HTTP, pkt-line, capability negotiation, sideband, relay framing
- [docs/convert-sha256.md](docs/convert-sha256.md) — one-off SHA1 → SHA256 repo conversion, mapping outputs, sharp edges
- [docs/testing.md](docs/testing.md) — test suites and integration coverage

## FAQ

### Does it sync complete Git history or only perform a shallow/partial sync?

`git-sync` syncs the complete Git object history required for the selected refs. It does not create a shallow clone. Some planning paths may use filtered fetches, but the target receives the full objects needed for valid refs.

### Is it just refs, or objects as well?

Objects as well. Refs are what `git-sync` plans and updates, but it also transfers the commits, trees, blobs, and tags needed for those refs to exist on the target.

### Is it bidirectional?

No. `git-sync` is one-way: source remote to target remote. To go the other way you'd run a second invocation with the endpoints swapped.

### Does it support create, update, and delete actions?

Yes. It supports creating refs, updating refs, force updates with `--force`, and deleting managed refs with `--prune`. `replicate` can overwrite target refs, but it is relay-only and more restrictive than `sync`.

### How does it scale?

`git-sync` has two transfer paths:

- **Relay** — pack data streams from source `upload-pack` directly into target `receive-pack`. The local process holds no object graph, so memory stays bounded regardless of repo size. Used when the target supports relay.
- **Materialized fallback** — when relay isn't available, `git-sync` fetches the needed objects into an in-memory `go-git` store, plans, then encodes and pushes a packfile. Memory scales with the diff being pushed and is guarded by an explicit object-count limit. Bootstrap can batch large initial syncs to keep this bounded.

Planning itself is cheap: ref-only round-trips, plus a `filter tree:0` fetch for ancestry checks when the source advertises filter support.

### How long does it take for a medium-sized repo?

It depends on repository size, network speed, and whether the relay path is available. As a rule of thumb, the relay path is bounded by source pack generation + network transfer + target receive-pack time — roughly the time of a `git clone` from the source plus a `git push` of the same pack. The materialized fallback adds local memory work for objects that need inspection.

For concrete numbers on your own setup, run the included benchmark tool against a representative repo; see [docs/testing.md](docs/testing.md#benchmarking).

### Does it support SSH?

Yes. `git-sync` supports SSH remotes through the local `ssh` binary, including
`ssh://`, SCP-style `git@host:path.git`, and `git+ssh://` URLs. See
[docs/usage.md](docs/usage.md) for details and current caveats.

### Does it run as a daemon or watch for changes?

No. `git-sync` is a one-shot CLI/library operation. To sync on a schedule or in response to events, run it from cron, CI, a worker, or another service.

## Contributing

See [CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md), [SECURITY.md](SECURITY.md), and [CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md](CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md).

input.command:
git add internal/gitproto/helper.go internal/gitproto/helper_test.go && git commit -q -F - <<'EOF'
Add git remote-helper transport (HelperConn)

Implement a gitproto.Conn backed by a git-remote-<scheme> binary, bridging
git-sync's smart transport onto the helper's stateless-connect capability.
This lets git-sync drive URL schemes it has no native transport for (e.g.
entire://) by delegating auth and network I/O to the helper while still
running the wire protocol itself.

Each Conn operation spawns its own helper process: the helper services one
stateless-connect session per process, and its receive-pack path reads the
pushed pack until stdin EOF, so a fresh process per RPC is the simplest
correct model and naturally supports batched (multi-request) pushes. The v2
upload-pack response is framed with a trailing response-end (0002) packet,
which is stripped so the consumer sees exactly the byte stream it would over
HTTP; receive-pack responses run to EOF. The helper strips the smart-HTTP
"# service=" banner, so advertisements match the bannerless SSH shape that
the existing decoders already accept.

Tests drive a fake helper via the os/exec re-exec pattern, covering the v2
advertisement, response-end stripping, receive-pack read-to-EOF, and the
fallback path, plus unit tests for the pkt-line framing helpers.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
git log --oneline -1

---
name: git-remote-entire-helper
description: Where the git-remote-entire helper lives and how its stateless-connect framing differs by service
metadata:
  type: reference
---

`entire://` URLs are handled by the `git-remote-entire` git remote helper.
Source: `../cli/internal/remotehelper/githelper/` (devenv/cli), notably
`stateless.go`, `connect.go`, `run.go`; the binary's main is
`../cli/cmd/git-remote-entire/`.

Key contract (confirmed by reading + probing): the helper's `stateless-connect`
handling differs by service. **upload-pack** uses true v2 stateless framing —
ack `\n`, advertisement, then per-request POST with a trailing response-end
(`0002`) packet. **receive-pack** falls through to `handleConnect` (a raw
proxy): ack `\n`, banner-stripped v0 advertisement, then it reads the push
request (commands + flush + pack-until-stdin-EOF), POSTs once, streams
report-status, and exits. So push needs one helper process per receive-pack
RPC. The helper owns auth via the CLI's context model (`~/.config/entire/
contexts.json` → keychain `entire-core:https://<auth-host>`), NOT the legacy
`hosts.json`/`entire:<host>` scheme.

git-sync consumes this via [[entire-backend-location]]'s sibling: see
`internal/gitproto/helper.go` (HelperConn) on the feat/remote-helper-transport
branch.

content.text:
Done. `entire://` (and any `git-remote-<scheme>`) now works natively in git-sync, validated against the live server.

## What I built

On branch `feat/remote-helper-transport`, three commits:

1. **`HelperConn` transport** (`internal/gitproto/helper.go` + tests) — a `gitproto.Conn` that spawns `git-remote-<scheme>`, drives its `stateless-connect` bridge, and runs the smart protocol over it. The helper owns auth and network I/O; git-sync still runs the wire protocol.
2. **Scheme dispatch** (`internal/syncer/syncer.go`) — `newConn` falls back to a remote helper for any non-native scheme, excluding http/https/ssh.
3. **Docs** — `docs/usage.md` + README FAQ.

## How it works (the key findings)

I read the helper's own source (`devenv/cli/internal/remotehelper/githelper/`) and probed it live. The `stateless-connect` framing differs by service:
- **upload-pack (fetch)**: true v2 stateless — ack `\n`, advertisement, then per-request POST with a trailing `0002` response-end packet (which `HelperConn` strips so the consumer sees exactly the HTTP byte stream).
- **receive-pack (push)**: a raw proxy that reads the whole push request until stdin EOF — so each RPC gets its own helper process, which also makes batched pushes work.

The advertisements come back banner-stripped (like SSH), which git-sync's existing decoders already accept.

## Validation

- `probe` of the `entire://` repo: authenticated via your `eu.auth.entire.io` context, negotiated **v2**, parsed capabilities. ✅
- Your exact original command: **`pushed=55008` refs, protocol=v2, relay-mode=bootstrap**, `refs/heads/main` and all checkpoints created. ✅
- `go build`, full `gitproto`/`syncer` tests, and `golangci-lint` (0 issues) all clean.

One inherited caveat I documented: like SSH, the helper bridge has no per-request byte counting, so `--stats` omits helper-side throughput (the GitHub side still printed `19.5 MB @ 2.89 MB/s`).

Nothing is pushed to a remote branch — the commits are local. Want me to open a PR, or add the efficiency optimization (reusing one process across the v2 ls-refs+fetch round trips instead of one-per-RPC)?

`/simplify → 4 cleanup agents in parallel → apply the fixes`

You are improving the quality of the changed code, not hunting for bugs. Review
it for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude issues, then fix what you
find. Do not look for correctness bugs — that is what `/code-review` is for.

## Phase 0 — Gather the diff

Run `git diff @{upstream}...HEAD` (or `git diff main...HEAD` / `git diff HEAD~1`
if there's no upstream) to get the unified diff under review. If there are
uncommitted changes, or the range diff is empty, also run `git diff HEAD` and
include the working-tree changes in scope — the review often runs before the
commit. If a PR number, branch name, or file path was passed as an argument,
review that target instead. Treat this diff as the review scope.

## Phase 1 — Review (4 cleanup agents in parallel)

Launch **4 independent review agents** via the Agent tool, all in a
single message so they run concurrently. Pass each agent the diff and one of
the four angles below. Each returns its findings with `file`, `line`, a
one-line `summary`, and the concrete cost (what is duplicated, wasted, or
harder to maintain).

### Reuse

Flag new code that re-implements something the codebase
already has — Grep shared/utility modules and files adjacent to the change,
and name the existing helper to call instead.

### Simplification

Flag unnecessary complexity the diff adds: redundant or derivable state,
copy-paste with slight variation, deep nesting, dead code left behind. Name
the simpler form that does the same job.

### Efficiency

Flag wasted work the diff introduces: redundant computation or repeated I/O,
independent operations run sequentially, blocking work added to startup or
hot paths. Also flag long-lived objects built from closures or captured
environments — they keep the entire enclosing scope alive for the object's
lifetime (a memory leak when that scope holds large values); prefer a
class/struct that copies only the fields it needs. Name the cheaper
alternative.

### Altitude

Check that each change is implemented at the right depth, not as a fragile
bandaid. Special cases layered on shared infrastructure are a sign the fix
isn't deep enough — prefer generalizing the underlying mechanism over adding
special cases.

## Phase 2 — Apply the fixes

Wait for all four agents to complete, dedup findings that point at the same
line or mechanism, and fix each remaining one directly. Skip any finding whose
fix would change intended behavior, require changes well outside the reviewed
diff, or that you judge to be a false positive — note the skip rather than
arguing with it. Finish with a brief summary of what was fixed and what was
skipped (or confirm the code was already clean).
I'll gather the diff and launch 4 parallel review agents.
